vix.note: VIX at 17.13, easing about 7 percent intraday after the post-FOMC spike to an 18.44 close on June 17. Volatility is elevated versus last week's 16.2 read as the Fed's late-2026 hike bias lifts rate uncertainty, but remains subdued in absolute terms.

macro: Hawkish Fed hold sinks megacap tech. The FOMC held the funds rate at 3.50-3.75 percent on June 17 in Kevin Warsh's debut meeting, but the updated dot plot lifted the 2026 year-end median to 3.8 percent, with 9 of 19 participants now penciling in at least one hike and traders moving to price a possible October move. Equities sold into the close (S&P 500 -1.2 percent, Nasdaq -1.3 percent), compounding a fragile AI-demand-scare tape that had already knocked NVDA about 8 percent and MSFT about 14 percent off early-June highs. Intel was the standout, jumping roughly 9 percent premarket on a Trump-announced Apple US-foundry deal, while gold steadied near 4,380 and oil kept sliding.
action: Stay defensive into a hawkish Fed: trim extended megacap-tech exposure, hold dry powder, and wait for SPY to confirm support near its 50-day (about 745) before adding broad risk.

long.h: Nvidia Prices $25B Bond Sale, Its First Since 2021
long.p: Nvidia priced a $25 billion investment-grade bond offering in mid-June, its first debt sale since the AI boom began, despite generating roughly $48.6 billion of free cash flow last quarter. The shift toward debt-funded capacity ('filling the barn before the storm') signals a changing capital structure as AI infrastructure spend scales.

long.h: Trump Says Apple Will Work With Intel to Build Chips in the US
long.p: President Trump said on Truth Social that Apple has agreed to work with Intel to design and manufacture chips in the United States, the biggest endorsement yet of Intel's foundry ambitions under CEO Lip-Bu Tan. If realized, an anchor customer of Apple's scale would materially de-risk the foundry build-out.

long.h: Fed Holds Rates but Dot Plot Now Signals a 2026 Hike
long.p: The FOMC held the funds rate at 3.50-3.75 percent for a fourth straight meeting, but raised the 2026 year-end median to 3.8 percent, with nine participants seeing at least one hike. Chair Warsh's debut statement was just three paragraphs, leaving the higher-for-longer signal to do the talking.

long.h: AI Hyperscalers' Capex Is Starting to Pay Off, Bulls Argue
long.p: Amazon, Microsoft and Alphabet each have core businesses strong enough to self-fund their data-center build-outs, and that spending is increasingly showing up in cloud revenue. The bull case frames the recent pullback as an entry point in the AI infrastructure leaders.

long.h: Strategists Warn 2026 Tape Is Starting to Look Like 1999
long.p: A growing chorus of analysts compares the AI boom's financing and IPO frenzy to the late-1990s bubble, warning that demand softening even briefly can wipe out large amounts of market value. One macro strategist likened GPU-backed financing to pre-2008 mortgage structures.

short.h: S&P and Nasdaq Slide as Fed Points to a Late-2026 Hike
short.p: Stocks sold off into the close on June 17, with the Nasdaq down 1.3 percent and the S&P 500 off 1.2 percent, after the Fed's projections revealed an increasing bias to raise rates later this year. Major indexes hit session lows in the final minutes.

short.h: Microsoft Falls Nearly 4% as Reported Talks Break Down
short.p: Microsoft closed at $378.86, down 3.8 percent and falling more steeply than the broader market, after reports that talks with another party broke down over a security matter (one side called the report inaccurate). The stock is now roughly 14 percent off its early-June high.

short.h: Meta Plummets Over 5% on Executive-Departure Report
short.p: Meta fell more than 5 percent on Wednesday following a report involving a long-tenured executive; the company declined to comment. The move pulled the stock back from its recent $628 peak toward $568.

short.h: Intel Jumps ~9% Premarket on Apple Chip-Deal Headlines
short.p: Intel shares rose as much as 9 percent in premarket and overnight trade after Trump's comments that Apple would partner with Intel on US chip design and manufacturing, adding to gains tied to expectations that SpaceX IPO cash could flow toward Intel.

short.h: Broadcom Upsizes Cash Tender for Outstanding Notes
short.p: Broadcom announced the expiration and upsized results of cash tender offers to buy back several series of senior notes, a liability-management step as the stock bounced 4.3 percent off its mid-month lows. AVGO remains well below its $480 early-June level after the AI-demand scare.

notes.NVDA: Nvidia at $204.65, down about 8 percent from its early-June high as the AI-demand scare and a hawkish Fed weigh on semis. Offsetting that: it priced a $25B bond (first since 2021) and sell-side still flags an 85 percent YoY revenue print and discounted multiple.
notes.MSFT: Microsoft at $378.91, off roughly 14 percent from early June after a 3.8 percent drop on reports that talks broke down over security. Azure growth and the renegotiated OpenAI deal underpin the long thesis; near-term sentiment is weak.
notes.INTC: Intel at $121.10 and up about 9 percent premarket on June 18 after Trump said Apple will work with Intel on US chip design and manufacturing. A genuine anchor foundry customer would be a major catalyst, but execution risk is high; treat as a speculative turnaround.
notes.AVGO: Broadcom at $392.90, bouncing 4.3 percent but still well below its $480 early-June level after a 7.9 percent AI-demand-scare drop. Company upsized a cash tender for outstanding notes; custom-silicon demand remains the structural driver.
notes.QQQ: Nasdaq-100 ETF at $722.51, off 1 percent as megacap tech led the post-FOMC selloff. The index sits just above its 50-day; a hawkish Fed and AI-bubble chatter cap near-term upside.
notes.SMH: Semis ETF at $623.97; volatile fortnight (intraday range from 570 to 647) as chip names whipsaw on AI-demand fears and rate jitters. Higher-beta exposure to the same selloff driving NVDA and AVGO.
notes.SPY: S&P 500 ETF at $740.96, down 1.2 percent on the day after the Fed signaled a possible late-2026 hike. Watch the 50-day near 745 as the line between consolidation and a deeper pullback.
notes.GOLD: Gold at $4,381, steadying after a dip to 4,114 mid-month; holding firm as a hedge while real-rate uncertainty rises post-FOMC. Still below the early-June 4,520 high.
notes.BTC: Bitcoin at $64,446, drifting down from 66k as risk sentiment cooled into the hawkish Fed. Trades as a high-beta risk asset here; rate uncertainty is the near-term headwind.
notes.AAPL: Apple at $295.95, relatively resilient (-1.1 percent); the reported Intel US-foundry partnership is a strategic positive for supply-chain reshoring. EU DMA scrutiny remains an overhang.
notes.AMZN: Amazon at $237.50, down 3.5 percent with the tape; AWS continues to absorb AI-infrastructure demand and bulls cite self-funded capex. Medium-term setup intact despite the pullback.
notes.META: Meta at $567.58, down more than 5 percent on an executive-departure report and now well off its $628 peak. AI-driven ad targeting is the core driver; near-term sentiment dented.
notes.GOOGL: Alphabet at $363.79, off 2.5 percent with peers; TPU roadmap and reported Intel foundry interest support the silicon-independence story. Among the steadier megacaps in the selloff.
notes.TSLA: Tesla at $396.38, down about 2 percent; SpaceX's mega-IPO keeps merger speculation alive. EV demand and valuation keep this high-risk despite the narrative.
notes.NFLX: Netflix at $76.96, breaking below its $80-86 range on a 2.2 percent slide. Ad tier and live content remain structural positives; momentum has turned soft.
notes.DIS: Disney at $100.86, holding up well (-0.4 percent) in a down tape; streaming nearing sustained profitability and Parks resilient. Defensive feel relative to tech.
notes.BRKB: Berkshire at $491.28, easing modestly but a clear relative outperformer in the selloff. Record cash pile and defensive mix make it the portfolio's ballast as rate risk rises.
notes.DIA: Dow ETF at $516.30; broad-market and value participation cushioning the tech-led drawdown. Less exposed to the AI de-rating than QQQ.
notes.SILVER: Silver at $70.77, recovering off a mid-month dip to 64; tracking gold higher but with more volatility given its industrial demand component.
notes.OIL: USO at $114.23, down hard from $140+ in early June on OPEC+ supply concerns and softer demand signals, only partly offset by Middle East risk. Underweight stance intact.
